chapters.title: Riches aren't just for the rich
chapters.text:
As they say, money cannot buy happiness, but it certainly can buy you some peace of mind. Think about it — in the United States alone, 66% of people find finances one of their most significant stressors. First, you stress about your university loan, then in 10 years, about your mortgage; in 20 years, you’re still in a rush to provide your children with a decent future, and in 40 years, you’re panic-stricken when you think about retirement. You’ve gotten stuck in a money-chasing race; on the horizon, you see a distant mirage — financial freedom.

If this is your case, do not despair. Financial freedom is not an optical illusion; it’s much more accessible than you can imagine. Statistics can back this up: eight out of ten millionaires come from below middle income, so what’s stopping you from becoming one of them?

Financial freedom is about breaking free from limiting beliefs, not just accumulating money.

Do you want to embrace a new vision of money and leave the paycheck-to-paycheck life behind? Then, this summary is your ticket to the life you have always imagined!

chapters.title: It's not your wallet, it's your ways
chapters.text:
There are no inherent "money issues" in the world. Those struggling to make ends meet might react with disbelief to this statement. However, let's reframe these as "money symptoms" instead. If you're experiencing financial difficulties, you can often trace their roots back to patterns and beliefs learned from your family.

Think back to your childhood. Did your parents ever tell you that money is wrong or dirty? If so, you might naturally run away from it. Or did you ever receive proper financial education from your parents? If not (which is the case for most people), you might be overly cautious with your finances and never take risks. The narrative you have about money shapes the way you earn it.

See money as a way to be happy and change the world, and notice how this perspective transforms your life.

People who struggle with money symptoms always shield themselves with excuses. Here are the most common ones:

• "I still have a lot of time/It’s too late now": For young people who want to stay carefree for a while — the earlier you start investing and saving money, the better. For those in their 40s and 50s — even though time isn't on your side, don't stay inactive; you can still improve your future.
• "I'll invest once I start making a lot of money": Your behavior, not the amount, defines your future wealth. If you invest $200 a month at an 8% rate for 30 years, you'll eventually make $300,000. $200 is just $50 per week, or $8 every day.
• "I must pay off my debt first": Carrying a financial burden and trying to invest simultaneously can seem overwhelming. But the key is to win time and form a behavioral pattern, so start small.
• "I am not smart enough to invest": Nobody is born financially savvy. You can learn the basics through books, courses, and videos or by hiring a mentor or financial advisor. Talk openly about money with friends and family, sharing experiences and insights.

Did you know? Only 14 states in the US have mandatory school courses on money.

chapters.title: Chasing dreams, not just dollars
chapters.text:
Have you ever sought the help of financial advisors and planners? If so, you may have noticed that they often start by examining your income and forming a plan for your future from there. This approach isn't entirely correct. It's better to first reflect deeply on your aspirations and dreams. Create a vision that feels so authentically "you" that it excites and motivates you every time you look at it. Grab your notebook and start forming it by following these steps:

Step 1: Craft your vision
To make the task easier, divide your life into domains. Here are some default domains: family and relationships, career, financial freedom, lifestyle, health and wellness, and legacy.

For each of these domains, ask three questions:
• What is your ideal scenario for this aspect of your life?
• What does this aspect mean to you?
• When you think about this domain, which words come to your mind?

Step 2: Want, Don't Want exercise
Divide your sheet into two columns, and write down things you want and do not want in your life. Be authentic and specific.

Step 3: Identify your money's purpose
Remember, money itself shouldn't be your ultimate aim in life. Instead, focus on what it can help you achieve. Start crafting your statement: "My money's purpose is…" Fill in the blank with what feels authentic to you.

Step 4: Make a contract with your future self
Imagine yourself in 10 years and write down your thoughts. This mental image will be your contract with yourself, which you must stay true to. Here are questions that will help you with that:

• In ten years, I see myself as…
• In ten years, I will contribute by…
• In ten years, I will love…
• In ten years, I'm surrounded by…
• In ten years, I've experienced…

You cannot find happiness in following society's script; it lies in pursuing your passions.

chapters.title: Get rich smart
chapters.text:
Value over income — this should be your mantra on your financial journey. Why is it so? While income is just the money you receive for your work, value is the expertise and knowledge you can offer. As your value increases, so does your income.

• Determine what you bring to the table: Identify your strong points that you can turn into money-making skills.
• Create a development plan: Develop a vision for improving your skills, including applying for courses or finding a mentor.
• Make sure your value aligns with your financial aspirations: Try to monetize the expertise and services you provide.
• Constantly review your value: Assess your contributions to ensure you continually add positively to your tasks. Check recent market trends to see if your skills are still relevant.

If you don't appreciate your value, it's unlikely that others will. Cherish qualities that make you unique.

If you are looking for practical ways to raise your value, try these methods:

• Receive formal education: Get a university degree or certificate from the training program; it can secure a well-paid position in a good company.
• Try on-the-job training: Research if your employer offers workshops or courses to enhance your expertise. Surprisingly, less than five percent of employees take advantage of this opportunity.
• Build your networks: Attend events and connect with people who share valuable insights regarding your field or any other sphere that interests you.
• Volunteer: Start volunteering in your community. Not only will you do good for the world, but you will also learn new skills.
• Self-direct your learning: The Internet offers resources like online courses, educational videos, and books that allow you to expand your expertise in any direction.

A rich life is built on the things that money can’t buy. ~ Mel H. Abraham

chapters.title: No risk, no story
chapters.text:
To build wealth, you must take risks; there’s no other option. Risking means, of course, investing. So, where do you begin? Here are several options you can explore:

• 401(k) plan: Many companies offer retirement plans for their employees. Suppose you contribute 4% of your salary to the plan; your employer might match 100%, so you're essentially getting free money.
• ESPP (employee stock purchase plan): As an employee, you can buy your company's stocks at a discount of 10-15%. It is an excellent alternative to traditional investing.
• HSA (health savings account): Of course, it's better to go through life without any significant health issues, but if they happen, HSA is there to help. The best part of this account is that money growth and withdrawals are tax-free. One strategy is not to withdraw money immediately, save the medical receipts, and wait for the funds to grow even without your contributing to the account (It works if funds aren't needed immediately). After age 65, you can withdraw money without proof of medical bills and penalty.
• Real estate: This option might work for people who have already accumulated a lot of money. If you have $50,000 and invest it in property, you'll only buy one house, which may not create significant cash flow for you. Getting out of real estate can also be challenging, as it takes much more time than selling stocks.

No matter your choice, you're on the right track as you try to make your money work for you. Here's an interesting fact: if you want to know how much time you need to double your invested money, use the Rule of 72. Divide 72 by the yearly interest rate. The result gives an estimate of how long it will take for your investment to double.

Begin investing as early as possible and make it a regular habit. Even small investments can grow over time.

chapters.title: Building a money machine for generations
chapters.text:
As you achieve your financial freedom, you can pass it down to future generations. With your money, you can leave a legacy that is both material, such as income or assets, and abstract, like financial knowledge and healthy money habits. One of the most valuable lessons you can pass on is how to build a "Money Machine" — a tool that generates wealth and provides financial stability. Here’s how you can create one:

Step 1: Establish the Money Machine foundation
Start with a well-thought-out financial plan that states the timeline, the numerous sources of income, and the tools you’ll use to achieve your goals. Focus on things like retirement savings, wealth accumulation, and educational funding.

Step 2: Work on your Money Machine components
There are three main elements of a Money Machine:
• Leveraged income streams: Some money sources require little effort to maintain, such as rental properties, dividend-paying stocks, and royalties. Try to get one or two of these sources for constant money flow.
• Growth assets: Put your money into investments like stocks and real estate properties that have potential to increase in value over time.
• Capital preservation strategies: Protect your wealth from issues like inflation and taxes. For this, invest in things that are inflation-secure and tax-efficient.

Protection is the final necessity of your affluence and legacy. ~ Mel H. Abraham

Step 3: Optimize the Money Machine
To keep yourself afloat, you must constantly check your financial plan and see how it corresponds to your aspirations. At the very beginning, revisit your plan once a quarter. Additionally, check the current trends and tools in the financial world to see what you can implement in your routine. Contact experienced financial coaches and tax advisors who can bring new perspectives to your financial planning.

Step 4: Make plans for passing your legacy
Ensure that your family has easy access to your finances upon your death. You can do so by creating a will or a trust that grants the rights to those you want to share your wealth with. Work out the succession plan if you own a business.

Remember to give back. Consider how your wealth can benefit your family and community.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Think of life as a cup of coffee. The coffee represents all the good moments and experiences we encounter. In contrast, the cup metaphorically represents everything that helps us contain that coffee — university, job, money, status. Some people focus on chasing shiny, glossy cups, forgetting that life is about savoring the coffee. Embrace this metaphor, and make money your tool, not the end purpose of your life.

Try this
• Make a habit of saving 20-25% of your monthly income to use for investing.
• Create an emergency fund with enough savings to cover your essential expenses for about three to six months.
• Use a tracker to constantly review your weekly and monthly incomes and expenses.

Read it till the end?
